title: Erythrophleum dinklagei
intro: Erythrophleum dinklagei, also known as Dinklage's coral tree, is a plant species native to the tropical regions of West Africa, belonging to the family Fabaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, malaria, and respiratory infections, due to its antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The bioactive constituents of Erythrophleum dinklagei include alkaloids, terpenoids, and flavonoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Erythrophleum dinklagei is often prepared as a decoction, infusion, or tincture, and is commonly used to treat various health conditions, including fever and respiratory infections.
taxonomy: Erythrophleum dinklagei is a species of flowering plant in the family Leguminosae. It is a part of the subfamily Caesalpinioideae and has been classified as a member of the genus Erythrophleum. The plant's classification reflects its close relationship with other Caesalpinioideae species. The classification of Erythrophleum dinklagei is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic analysis. Erythrophleum dinklagei is often studied in the context of its unique characteristics and its place within the larger Leguminosae family.

side_effects: Erythrophleum dinklagei is a plant that has been associated with toxicity and is known to cause severe side effects. The seeds of the plant contain a toxic compound called erythrophleine, which can be fatal if ingested.
